item.title: Technical articles
item.description: BI.ZONE expert research and practical materials on cybersecurity

item.title: A striking resemblance: Gambling Hyena and Twelfth Hyena clusters compared
item.description: What is common between two hacktivist groups attacking the Russian government sector

item.title: Rare Wolf preys on sensitive data using fake 1C:Enterprise invoices as a lure
item.description: How adversaries create diversions and stay invisible

item.title: Mysterious Werewolf attacks Russian industry
item.description: What is known about the WinRAR vulnerability that the group is exploiting and how to recognize the attacks

item.title: A research of attacks on public organizations involving malware-as-a-service tools
item.description: How weak defenses allow the spies to operate without sophisticated tools

item.title: White Snake is weaponized against Russian companies
item.description: Criminals leverage White Snake in phishing emails to steal corporate data. Such attacks do not demand much skill: all that the intruder needs is to rent out the stealer. The program's low cost and simple operation make it highly demanded on the darknet

item.title: New hacker group Quartz Wolf leverages legitimate software to attack the hospitality/hotel industry
item.description: Own malware has never been the go-to for cybercriminals. The Quart Wolf hacker group has devised another way to bypass conventional defenses. By using the Assistant remote access software the group attempts to compromise its target systems. In this publication, we reveal the mechanics of this attack and share how BI.ZONE CESP was able to detect and prevent it

item.title: Red Wolf is back to spy on commercial firms
item.description: Red Wolf, the hacker group that went off the radar in 2022, is back with its old tricks, leveraging classic phishing ploys to penetrate corporate infrastructures. Aiming to remain invisible as long as possible, the group breaks down its intrusion into stages to bypass conventional defenses

item.title: Core Werewolf targets the defense industry and critical infrastructure
item.description: Amid the changing environment, Core Werewolf is one of the new cybercriminal groups actively engaged in espionage. The first attacks attempted by the adversaries can be traced back to 2021. They target Russian organizations associated with the defense industry and critical information infrastructure

item.title: Malware: everybody gets one. How open-source software steals corporate passwords
item.description: Stealers, malicious programs that target user credentials, have been around for a long time and are still popular among cybercriminals. In this article, we will look into the mechanics of Umbral, a new stealer that has been used against a number of Russian companies. Uniquely enough, the source code for Umbral is available to anyone at GitHub

item.title: Watch Wolf targets accountants through SEO poisoning
item.description: Delivering malware through phishing emails has become a standard practice among cybercriminals. The Watch Wolf hacker group, however, has opted for a new approach. Using search engine optimization, the group promotes fraudulent websites to lure unsuspecting accountants to install a backdoor on their computers

item.title: Destructive attacks by Key Wolf: how to spot the new ransomware
item.description: BI.ZONE's cyber intelligence unit reports on an attack campaign engineered by the Key Wolf hacker group. Aimed specifically at the users in Russia, the attack prompts them to launch malware and thus trigger file encryption on their computers. Interestingly enough, the attackers do not demand any ransom

item.title: Vulnerabilities in J-Link licensing system, or Why researching device security matters
item.description: Vulnerabilities in hardware are not easy to find and sometimes are impossible to fix. However, security testing of IoT gadgets, smartphones, tablets, and other devices should not be left behind

item.title: Our New Log4j Scanner to Combat Log4Shell
item.description: The scanner will help find applications that are using the vulnerable Log4j library

item.title: Insecure deserialization
item.description: This section covers the notion of JSON Web Tokens, what they consist of, how they are exploited for user authentication and what the advantages are of JWT compared with a classic authentication scheme using sessions

item.title: Venture Wolf attempts to disrupt Russian businesses with MetaStealer
item.description: Facts about a newly detected threat actor and how to spot its attacks

item.title: Exploring CVE-2024-38227 vulnerability in Microsoft SharePoint
item.description: We investigated third-party code execution in the corporate portal solution and also analyzed its architecture

item.title: Core Werewolf hones its arsenal against Russia's government organizations
item.description: Adversaries experiment with new tools and malware delivery methods

item.title: Wreaking havoc in cyberspace: threat actors experiment with pentest tools
item.description: A new research by BI.ZONE Threat Intelligence reveals how adversaries attempt to bypass cybersecurity systems

item.title: Zooming in on CVE-2024-7965
item.description: We have analyzed the CVE-2024-7965 vulnerability that allows adversaries to execute arbitrary code in the Google Chrome renderer

item.title: Stone Wolf employs Meduza Stealer to hack Russian companies
item.description: A new cluster of activity abuses a legitimate brand to spearphish for credentials and system data

item.title: Breaking down Windows critical vulnerability CVE-2024-38063
item.description: We take a closer look at the Windows TCP/IP network stack flaw that could potentially lead to a full system compromise without any action on the part of the user

item.title: Bloody Wolf strikes organizations in Kazakhstan with STRRAT commercial malware
item.description: The program selling for as little as $80 on underground resources allows the adversaries to take control of corporate computers and hijack restricted data

item.title: Sapphire Werewolf polishes Amethyst stealer to attack over 300 companies
item.description: The adversaries use the open-source SapphireStealer to create their own malware for collecting employee authentication data from Russian companies

item.title: Scaly Wolf's new loader: the right tool for the wrong job
item.description: The BI.ZONE Threat Intelligence team has uncovered a fresh campaign by the group targeting Russian and Belarusian organizations

item.title: Cloud Werewolf spearphishes for government employees in Russia and Belarus with fake spa vouchers and federal decrees
item.description: The attackers use phishing emails with seemingly legitimate documents and evade defenses by hosting the malicious payload on a remote server and limiting its downloads

item.title: Fluffy Wolf sends out reconciliation reports to sneak into corporate infrastructures
item.description: The group has adopted a simple yet effective approach to gain initial access: phishing emails with an executable attachment. This way, Fluffy Wolf establishes remote access, steals credentials, or exploits the compromised infrastructure for mining

item.title: Mysterious Werewolf hits defense industry with new RingSpy backdoor
item.description: The criminal group gains initial access through phishing emails with a compressed executable that unleashes RingSpy, an original remote access backdoor

item.title: Scaly Wolf uses White Snake stealer against Russian industry
item.description: The group has been on the radar since the summer of 2023, conducted several phishing campaigns, using Russian state body and law enforcement identities

item.title: Digging into CVE-2025-62592 in Oracle VirtualBox for macOS
item.description: We uncover a virtualization software vulnerability causing an ASLR leak
